posts.author: cvs
posts.text: US broker restrictions on SpaceX IPO investors — While this is logical, and sounds reasonable, … One implication of “letting market forces decide” is accepting that the system will fall prey to the tragedy of commons . In other words, refusing to regulate is also a way of regulating biasing the system towards a pre-determined outcome. One practical challenge would be getting everyone involved to arrive at a common single definition of “arbitrary”. How to agree upon what’s arbitrary and what is not? This tangent of thought has been explored to some extent in this previous topic-thread…

posts.author: BB789
posts.text: US broker restrictions on SpaceX IPO investors — Making market forces decide the rules is wiser than arbitrary rules. Flippers add no value except blanket opening selling pressure. If SEBI is writing the rules, it should do the same: prefer allotment to keepers instead of a lottery system allotment where flippers are made equally eligible.

posts.author: nithin_kumrr
posts.text: US broker restrictions on SpaceX IPO investors — Now, the restrictions. Some U.S. brokers actively discourage investors from selling allotted IPO shares soon after listing. Check the restriction. Fidelity can restrict access to future IPO allocations for 6 months if allocated shares are sold within 15 calendar days. Second offense is a 1 year ban. Third offense is a permanent ban tied to your Social Security number. Robinhood can restrict access to future IPO allocations for 60 days if allocated shares are sold within 30 days. E*Trade says selling allocated IPO shares within 30 days can affect…

posts.author: nithin_kumrr
posts.text: US broker restrictions on SpaceX IPO investors — So here’s the story. SpaceX came to market at a valuation of roughly $1.77 trillion. Within hours of trading, it crossed $2 trillion. The company sold 555.6 million shares at $135 each, raising $75 billion. Saudi Aramco’s $29.4 billion in 2019 was the previous record. This is nearly three times that. Total demand crossed $350 billion, making the offering roughly 4.5x oversubscribed. Retail side alone is $100 billion against maybe $15 to 17 billion available. The demand for the SpaceX IPO was far higher than the shares available. Many investors g…

posts.author: nithin_kumrr
posts.text: US broker restrictions on SpaceX IPO investors — SpaceX crossed $2 trillion within hours of listing. The valuation is one thing, but some of the broker rules around IPO shares are downright bizarre. Quoting @nithin 's tweet from today. Sure, things can be better, but it’s crazy how transparent and safe the Indian markets are compared to the US, all thanks to SEBI and the exchanges. posts.author: bearishbhaiya
posts.text: SpaceX IPO and forced index inclusion — SpaceX lists on June 12 at a $1.75 trillion valuation. It’s the largest IPO in history. But the more interesting story now. All three major index providers changed their rules this year to let SpaceX in faster. Nasdaq cut its inclusion window from 90 trading days to 15. FTSE Russell cut theirs to 5 days. S&P 500 is proposing to waive the profitability rule it has held since 2002, and halve the 12 month listing requirement. SpaceX is loss making on an accounting basis and is only offering 3-5% of its shares to the public. Under the old rules it would not…
